author: @Johny40Se7en

That is truly brilliant, however, I'm just thinking how the Oceans are in one heck of a state already, in no small part because of an immense load of throwaway plastic crud discarded by the fishing industry which people blithely support when they buy fish products, and everything else which is chucked away, therefore, if loads of people start harvesting a ton of seaweed it's likely going to be even worse for the overall health of the oceans.

What about adding thick liquid corn starch to the hemp matting, and grinding up fruit peelings, such as orange and mango, and banana and coconut fibres to give it even more strength. If anyone's got a good kiln ready to go, give it a try 😉

author: @flashkraft
text:
This is similar to Hempwood created by a company in Kentucky USA. They compress Hemp into panels and beams and then seal it all in using a Soy based resin.
Basically what you are doing is fibre glassing but using hemp instead of glass fibre and natural resins to glue it together instead of epoxy polyester.

6:16 - "I hope it sparks ideas in you"
My brain: Yes. Every. Single. Time!
Thank you so much for focusing on these platforms of innovation - I'll be building an odd capacitor-solenoid-thingy soon, due to another one of your videos. And, I was already looking at ceramic plates that assemble into honeycombed surfaces; forming the bucky-triangles of a geodome's mosaic with them laid edge-to-edge to make a deep, hollowed prism of flanges, same principle for support as an I-beam, extending to interior domes for architectural space-frames - so, you said "if you can make a flat panel, you can make anything." Definitely!

I had a lecturer that was really into using woven cellulose fibres with a cellulose matrix and demonstrated a skateboard that was made. His market was European car industry where you had to recycle or dispose of parts responsibly. 
The fibres are available through the tyre making process, and the matrix is, well, wallpaper glue. His name was dr. Mark Steiger,  University of Canterbury, NZ

author: @stoatrepublic
text:
Henry Ford made a Car body from Hemp, it was reported as been stronger then a steel body and was tested in a promotional video with a sledge hammer.  He was told he wouldn't be allowed to make production cars.
In the video the rear corner of the test vehicle is repeatedly hammered and the panel retains its orginal shape.   The reason Henry was using hemp plastic was because he was already growing Hemp on his farm to produce hemp based fuel for his cars, while in the meantime Henry's wife was driving a Baker electric car.

author: @justinw1765
text:
Dang it Robert, you read my mind again...  Yesterday, before I saw your first video on this, I went to the store to get tapioca starch for the express purpose to make bio plastic with it.  And, I was thinking to myself, "I have some woven flax linen fabric that would make a great substrate for this."  

     Unfortunately, I do not live close to the sea anymore (wish I did as I love Her), but I do live near an Asian food market and Agar-Agar is pretty cheap there.  I also plan to do a minority blend of the agar to the  starch.  I was also planning on experimenting with adding crystalline nanocellulose to it to strengthen it, but I will probably will need to add some extra glycerin since that stuff increases stiffness.  A little goes a long way with CNC.

  Also thinking about trying out some beeswax and/or shea butter in there to increase hydrophobicity.  I imagine the more wax one adds, the less glycerin they will need.  After all, that stretchy, parafilm lab stuff is basically just PE and parafin wax mixed together.  
  Cheers

author: @Kyle-sg4rm
text:
What do you think about making a gel, or more solid state battery using these types of simple accessible bio-plastics? I have done some research on similar things and I and figure that using it for electrode/electrolyte material could potentially be safe and effective. 
I thought maybe a range of metal could be tested, including oxide, acetate, chloride, forms. Ideally it would be simple homemade stuff (probably somewhat impure) like Ferric acetate, Potassium carbonate, Sodium acetate, Calcium acetate, Calcium chloride, etc. Able to be Produced from scratch with stuff like wood ashes/seaweed ashes, seashells/snail-shells/limestone, black sand/old nails, vinegar, sea salt and so on.
I've had some trouble finding out if any of these bio-plastics could somehow be used as a battery separator material as well as electrode/electrolyte material - If you know about it, or could point me in the right direction, that would be great.
I will be experimenting on this stuff myself, but I saw your video and thought I should mention it as it might appeal to you and the viewers. You may want to extend the scope of available materials and also include things like graphene, etc. I was just thinking of what might be possible with what is already present in the rural/wild environment. Stuff that's free, natural and available mostly everywhere.
Thanks - Kyle

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you can certainly use it to gel the electrolyte but beyond that - I don't know really - very interesting question

replies.author: @Kyle-sg4rm
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering I wonder if that casein + tannic acid rubber stuff that you did a video on might allow ions to pass through? It appears to have similar hydrophobic properties to rubber, but is it an insulator? Food for thought. Thanks for making these interesting videos and for advising people to also do their own research on things!

author: @Alwis-Haph-Rytte
text:
Knock knock
"Who is it?"
"Police social worker"
"What do you want?"
"It's about the Marijuana you have growing"
"Nah, that's Hemp"
"Marijuana is Spanish for Hemp, their both Cannabis Sativa"
"No, this is for bio plastic"
"Oh, your saying it's Agri Hemp?"
"Yeah"
"Gotta permit?"
"No"
"Did you know growing Agri Hemp spaced out planting will raise the THC above what is allowed for Agri Hemp which makes it Marijuana?"
"Uh, no"
"What's the seaweed for?"
"You believe it's not for smoking?"

author: @jwioo
text: I have been working on organic low density composites for some time, some of the biggest issues are making them water proof and prevention of mold growth but I have found that an ordinary household iron is quite effective at flattening out thin sheets.

replies.author: @TheKlink
replies.text: What about adding water glass to the mix? Or painting it with water glass once dry?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Rick Simpson   It won't be particularly environmentally friendly if you add those.  But some beeswax or shea butter might work.  Klink's suggestion is definitely worth trying out.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: just paint it mate - as for mould stick some disinfectant in

replies.author: @jwioo
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks for the responses guys, I have solved the mold problem by adding Listerene mouth wash. For waterproofing I have been experimenting with gelatine, with some success. I had thought of waterglass but because the filler material is so soft (pith from elder trees) the binder needs to be relatively weak or when machined the filler pulls out. The most successful binder to date has been corn starch and hot water.

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text:
You can add borax (20 miles team laundry additive) or boric acid (some insecticides) to your resin/glue for mold resistance ... and many insects as well... It may even add some flame resistance.
Neem oil works o.k. too if it's oil based and you want a "natural" alternative.

author: @infinitelyuniversal2390
text:
BRILLIANT! So what happened to Henry Ford making an all Hemp plastic vehicle, that was stronger then and lighter then steel apparently!  

Such an old ideology that  has never fully taken off!

I love how you are all about DIY and our world is only interested in Profiting and discarding!

My favorite composite material is denim jean material, you can soak it any kind of adhese or paints  stretch it out during curing process and makes a super strong plastic like material.  Lots of fun making seatcovers that outlast leather or pleather!

Good example take a flat shoes lace, soak in crazy or super glue, and stretch the shoe lace string very tight in a vice, and when cured you will have super strong light weight peice of plastic that is spring like stiff, i tried bending completely in half and bounces right back to shape*

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Speaking of carbon fibre, apparently Titebond III makes a decent epoxy and polyester resin replacement.  It's much cheaper and much less toxic as well.  It has a tensile/PSI strength of 4000, which is comparable to polyester resin and some two part epoxies (there are some epoxies that have twice as much, but they are quite expensive).  

   Also, if you want to make your own super materials, check out crystalline nanocellulose--and especially the carbonized versions of same.  The latter has higher tensile and stiffness strengths than carbon fiber--much more than the easily available to the consumer types.  
  I've thinking that also woven bamboo would make a really good substrate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - and I would love to see a video of this

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text:
You can get fiberglass mats in most hardware stores with a welding or plumbing section.  Burlap bags might work o.k. too, but even old T-shirts work.  You can also use gelatin to make a type of hide-glue that allows repair simply by adding heat (typical for string instruments)
If you boil cornstarch with a bit of sodium hydroxide (in many drain cleaners) it makes a really strong glue.  Adding boric acid (in some insecticides) to a thick solution of it will make a moldable plastic that sets pretty hard and is extremely heat resistant (adding baking soda increases heat resistance)

author: @jimparsons6803
text: With respect, plastic has a chemical definition --- repeating monomers.  I don't think that the flat board that he makes qualifies under the chemical definition.  Given the presented evidence, most of the cooked food that folks eat would be a "plastic."

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Clearly you've never had my cooking, as most of it could indeed be considered "plastic".  😋  But I believe the "plastic" in this case would be the seaweed mixture.  I'd recommend watching his video on how to make it, as I believe it fits the definition you're looking for.  Here, the hemp is merely used to reinforce the seaweed-based plastic to create a composite material, which I believe is the whole point of this video (i.e. how to use it to make composite materials).

replies.author: @BurnabyAlex
replies.text: Not all plastics are polymers, and not everyone is limited to a recent chemists interpretation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: what we are using here are polysaccharides  - that fits your definition for sure - but there are two understood uses for 'plastic' one is the one you gave and the other is the commonly understood term - and to be honest I agree with you most of what we commonly describe as plastic is far from it but are mostly composites - even what you think of as not a composite usually is - an injection folded HDPE bowl for example is mostly a blend of filler, colour, recycled HDPE and vergin HDPE pellets - making it strictly a composite - but do we need to be that anal? lol - and you are right most of the food we eat is a plastic by the chemical definition but then our bodies are built from 'plastics' by that definition. It would have been nice if you addressed me personally - cheers mate
